title: TinyStories: How Small Can Language Models Be and Still Speak Coherent English?
claim: Models below 10M params (even one transformer block) generate fluent, consistent multi-paragraph stories on a 3-4-year-old-vocabulary synthetic corpus, where GPT-2/GPT-Neo-small (~125M) on ordinary data rarely produce coherent text; evaluation is GPT-4-graded (grammar/creativity/consistency).

title: Findings of the BabyLM Challenge: Sample-Efficient Pretraining on Developmentally Plausible Corpora
claim: >30 submissions on 10M/100M-word tracks; LTG-BERT architecture won; curriculum-learning submissions 'largely unsuccessful, though some showed modest improvements'; other wins from shorter sequences and knowledge distillation. (Findings content fetched via the arXiv version 2504.08165; anthology page = venue metadata.)

title: Findings of the Second BabyLM Challenge: Sample-Efficient Pretraining on Developmentally Plausible Corpora
venue: arXiv (CoNLL 2024 per EVAL.sources; not restated on abs page)
claim: 31 submissions; hybrid causal+masked (GPT-BERT) winner; best submissions changed data + objective + architecture; 'a strong relationship between training FLOPs and average performance across tasks'; no submission beat baselines on the multimodal track.

title: GPT or BERT: why not both?
claim: Hybrid masked+causal pretraining (GPT-BERT) outperforms masked-only and causal-only models on BabyLM 2024; resulting model usable transparently as either causal or masked LM; models/corpora/code released.

title: modded-nanogpt: NanoGPT speedrun (GPT-2 124M-quality on FineWeb, 8xH100)
claim: IMMUTABLE PROVENANCE PIN: repo commit edf47a05a12062d661c4cfd4eef848c5ab5bed32 (master HEAD of 2026-07-03; README re-fetched at that exact commit 2026-07-11). README at pinned commit documents 84 records since 2024-05-28; latest record #84 = 1.32 min (2026-05-21) to val loss <=3.28 on FineWeb vs the llm.c 45-min baseline (~34x wall-clock) on the leaderboard's fixed 8xH100 hardware/software configuration; records require enough run logs for p<0.01 statistical significance that mean val loss <=3.28. Techniques accumulated ACROSS records (bundled; per-component contributions unmeasured): Muon/NorMuon optimizer, rotary, QK-norm, ReLU^2, FlashAttention-3 long-short sliding windows, FP8/bf16 precision, multi-token prediction, value/bigram-hash embeddings. Wall-clock engineering record on one hardware config — NOT a training-FLOP comparison and NOT evidence a plain GPT-2 twin is 34x less compute-efficient.

title: Muon is Scalable for LLM Training
claim: With weight decay + per-parameter update-scale added, Muon achieves ~2x computational efficiency vs AdamW at compute-optimal training; scales to Moonlight 3B/16B-MoE on 5.7T tokens; distributed implementation + checkpoints released.

title: SmolLM2: When Smol Goes Big -- Data-Centric Training of a Small Language Model
claim: 1.7B model trained on ~11T tokens (~6,470 tok/param, ~325x Chinchilla-optimal) via multi-stage data mixing (FineMath, Stack-Edu, SmolTalk); outperforms Qwen2.5-1.5B and Llama3.2-1B. Load-bearing use here: released small anchors are massively overtrained artifacts, not matchable twins.

title: Pythia: A Suite for Analyzing Large Language Models Across Training and Scaling
venue: ICML (oral; icml.cc virtual page surfaced this pass)
claim: 16 LLMs 70M-12B trained on identical public data in identical order; 154 checkpoints each; reconstructible dataloaders — the controlled-suite/model-organism practice for twin construction.

title: 2 OLMo 2 Furious
claim: Fully open 7B/13B/32B models (weights+data+code+recipes+logs+checkpoints); training-stability recipe changes and late-stage curriculum 'Dolmino Mix 1124' mid-training; claimed Pareto frontier of performance-to-training-compute among open models.

title: MobileLLM: Optimizing Sub-billion Parameter Language Models for On-Device Use Cases
claim: At 125M/350M, the COMBINED MobileLLM design package (deep-thin shape + embedding sharing + grouped-query attention) improves +2.7/+4.3 pp average accuracy over prior same-size models; block-wise weight sharing adds +0.7/+0.8. The pp figures are model-level package-vs-prior-models improvements, NOT single-factor causal estimates of deep-thin-vs-wide-shallow; the paper's internal design exploration supports deep-thin directionally within its own setting.

title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
claim: Loss is power-law in N, D, C with trends spanning >7 orders of magnitude; width/depth shape has minimal impact within tested ranges; recommends large models on modest data stopped before convergence (later corrected by Chinchilla/Porian).

title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla)
venue: arXiv (NeurIPS 2022 commonly credited; venue not restated on abs page this pass)
claim: 400+ models 70M-16B, 5-500B tokens: compute-optimal N and D scale equally (~20 tok/param); Chinchilla 70B (4x data) beats Gopher 280B at same compute, MMLU 67.5% (+7 over Gopher); isoFLOP profiles = approach 2.

title: Chinchilla Scaling: A replication attempt
claim: Hoffmann et al. approach-3 parametric fit: inconsistent with approaches 1-2, poor fit to extracted data, implausibly narrow CIs ('would require over 600,000 experiments' vs <500 run); authors' re-derivation reconciles with approaches 1-2. Load-bearing use: published scaling-law FITS are fragile.

title: Resolving Discrepancies in Compute-Optimal Scaling of Language Models
claim: Kaplan-vs-Chinchilla exponent discrepancy explained by three methodology factors: last-layer FLOP accounting, warmup duration, scale-dependent optimizer tuning; with corrections, excellent agreement with Chinchilla; careful LR decay NOT essential; AdamW beta2 tuning essential at small batch sizes.

title: Tensor Programs V: Tuning Large Neural Networks via Zero-Shot Hyperparameter Transfer (muTransfer)
claim: Under maximal-update parametrization (muP) many optimal HPs are stable across width, enabling zero-shot HP transfer: beat published BERT-large by tuning a 13M proxy; outperformed GPT-3 6.7B tuning from a 40M proxy at ~7% of pretraining cost.

title: Tensor Programs VI: Feature Learning in Infinite-Depth Neural Networks (Depth-muP)
claim: Depth-muP gives depthwise HP transfer for single-layer residual blocks, but 'fundamental limitations in all possible infinite-depth limits' when each block is deeper (modern transformers) — depth transfer does NOT work as cleanly as width; sweep on width, hold depth.

title: Small-scale proxies for large-scale Transformer training instabilities
venue: arXiv (ICLR 2024 commonly credited; venue not restated on abs page this pass)
claim: Large-scale instabilities (attention-logit growth, output-logit divergence) reproduce in small models at high learning rates; large-scale mitigations (QK-norm, warmup, weight decay, muParam) equally effective there; activation/gradient-norm scaling can predict instabilities before they fire.

title: Language models scale reliably with over-training and on downstream tasks
venue: ICLR 2025 (proceedings.iclr.cc paper file surfaced this pass); arXiv 2024
claim: 104-model testbed 0.011B-6.9B, 3 data distributions: scaling laws extrapolate across over-training amounts — predicted 1.4B/900B-token (32x overtrained) and 6.9B/138B runs from 300x-less-compute experiments; perplexity->downstream top-1-error power law at 20x less compute.

title: Scaling Data-Constrained Language Models
venue: NeurIPS (oral; neurips.cc virtual page surfaced this pass)
claim: Up to 900B tokens / 9B params on natural web-scale distributions: training with up to ~4 epochs of repeated data comes close to unique-data performance (paper wording: 'negligible changes to loss'); with more repetition the value of added compute decays toward zero; data-constrained compute-optimality scaling law proposed and validated. Scope: the tested regimes only — NOT a blanket <=4-epoch 'no-penalty' license for arbitrary synthetic/tiny corpora.

title: Cramming: Training a Language Model on a Single GPU in One Day
venue: arXiv (ICML 2023 commonly credited; venue not restated on abs page this pass)
claim: Fixed one-GPU-day budget reaches near-BERT performance; re-analysis of the full pretraining pipeline under fixed compute; 'performance closely follows scaling laws observed in large-compute settings' even at tiny budgets.

title: DataDecide: How to Predict Best Pretraining Data with Small Experiments
claim: 25 corpora x scales up to 1B params/100B tokens, 3 seeds, 30k checkpoints: ranking at a single small scale (150M) predicts best-at-1B in ~80% of comparisons (~20% flip at 8x scale); NONE of 8 scaling-law extrapolation baselines beat single-scale ranking; continuous likelihood metrics best proxies (>80% predictable at 0.01% of target compute). Domain: PRETRAINING-DATA selection between standard-architecture models at ~150M->1B — NOT symbolic-interface interventions, architecture changes, 5-30M models, or signed treatment-effect trends; transfers to the kot sweep protocol by analogy only.

title: Broken Neural Scaling Laws
claim: Smoothly-broken power laws model non-monotonic scaling (double descent, sharp inflections); seemingly smooth scaling curves may contain hidden breaks — extrapolating from few small-scale points carries inherent uncertainty.

title: Loss-to-Loss Prediction: Scaling Laws for All Datasets
claim: Shifted power laws translate train-to-train, train-to-test, and test-to-test losses across data distributions; predictions extrapolate well at 20x the largest fitted FLOP budget; sometimes beats single-dataset scaling-law extrapolation.

title: Improving language models by retrieving from trillions of tokens (RETRO)
venue: arXiv (ICML 2022 commonly credited; venue not restated on abs page this pass)
claim: Trained-in chunked cross-attention over a 2T-token retrieval store: comparable Pile performance to GPT-3/Jurassic-1 at 25x fewer parameters — a comparison explicitly NOT resource- or lifecycle-matched (the 2T-token store sits outside the parameter count); not evidence that interfaces help at every scale. (Per-scale gain range from the paper body not re-checked this pass; only the abstract claim is used.) Already in lit-KB as arxiv_2112.04426 (seam record).

title: Generalization through Memorization: Nearest Neighbor Language Models (kNN-LM)
claim: Post-hoc kNN interpolation in a trained LM's embedding space, NO additional training: Wikitext-103 SOTA perplexity 15.79 (-2.9) in the single tested configuration. Load-bearing use: prior art that a non-learned interface can help without host retraining IN ITS TESTED CONFIGURATION — one configuration is NOT evidence that interfaces help regardless of host capacity or at every scale. Already in lit-KB as arxiv_1911.00172 (seam record).

title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
venue: NeurIPS 2023 (openreview Yacmpz84TH surfaced this pass; abs page silent)
claim: Self-supervised API-call learning (GPT-J 6.7B base). Scale sweep on GPT-2 family 124M/355M/775M/1.6B: 'the ability to leverage the provided tools only emerges at around 775M parameters; smaller models achieve similar performance both with and without tools' (exception: the easy Wikipedia-search API) — figure from paper body via PDF excerpt located this pass. Within-ONE-lineage observation in which scale co-varies with base capability, generated-call quality, filtering, and task mix: prior evidence of a POSSIBLE capacity threshold for learned interface use, not a general measured interface threshold.

title: Textbooks Are All You Need (phi-1)
claim: 1.3B model, 7B tokens (6B filtered 'textbook-quality' web + 1B GPT-3.5-synthetic): HumanEval 50.6 pass@1, MBPP 55.5; 350M variant reaches 45% HumanEval — the flagship data-quality-beats-scale claim (consume only with a decontamination audit; see phi-ctnl-satire-2023).

title: Pretraining on the Test Set Is All You Need
claim: Satirical 1M-param 'phi-CTNL' achieves perfect benchmark scores by pretraining on the benchmarks: data-quality/emergence claims from curated corpora are uninterpretable without contamination audits.

title: The MultiBERTs: BERT Reproductions for Robustness Analysis
claim: 25 BERT-base runs identical except seed (init + data shuffle): 'repeating the pre-training process can lead to substantially different performance' downstream; introduces the Multi-Bootstrap for inference over multiple pretrained models with limited test data; 25 final + 140 intermediate checkpoints released.

title: Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?
claim: Apparent emergence is largely an artifact of nonlinear/discontinuous metrics; under continuous metrics the same models improve smoothly. CARRIED from docs/next/lit/EVAL.md sect-5 (verified there 2026-07-11); UNVERIFIED-THIS-PASS.

title: Observational Scaling Laws and the Predictability of Language Model Performance
claim: ~100 public models define a low-dimensional capability space in which emergent phenomena, agent performance, and post-training technique value follow smooth predictable sigmoids. CARRIED from docs/next/lit/EVAL.md sect-5 (verified there 2026-07-11); UNVERIFIED-THIS-PASS.
